ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for Machli Amritsari; closest parent is fried fish food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Machli Amritsari; A0CKP (fish, battered/breaded, deep-fried) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Machli Amritsari or Amritsari fried fish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Machli Amritsari consists of approximately 150 g of deep-fried spiced fish pieces, usually served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, fried in batter — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for fried fish in batter (besan-coated). Machli Amritsari is a Punjabi preparation of deep-fried battered fish; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN fried fish entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for fish, battered, fried (FDC ID 175168) to confirm plausibility. Serving size set at 150 g based on typical restaurant portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Machli Amritsari. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous deep-fried battered fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Battered fried fish has a low GI (approximately 35–40) due to the protein and fat content slowing glucose absorption; gram flour (besan) batter has a lower GI than wheat flour. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 16.8) / 100 ≈ 6.4 for a 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~630 mg per 150 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments, as a standard serving contributes approximately 27% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~38) and moderate carbohydrate content (~11 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Machli Amritsari has a low GI and moderate carbohydrate load, making it a relatively suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (100–150 g) and without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying increases total fat and caloric density; however, fish provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the deep-frying method elevates total fat (~16.8 g per 100 g) and caloric density, the underlying fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100 g, prefer preparations with less oil, and monitor overall saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Machli Amritsari is a classic Punjabi deep-fried fish dish originating from Amritsar, a city in the Punjab region of northern India. The dish consists of boneless fish pieces — traditionally sole, basa, or singhara (catfish) — marinated in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter seasoned with ginger, green chillies, lemon juice, carom seeds (ajwain), turmeric, red chilli powder, and garam masala, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. The result is a dish with a crunchy, spiced exterior and a moist, flaky interior, delivering bold flavours characteristic of Punjabi street food. It is commonly served as a starter or snack with mint-coriander chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. In Singapore, it appears on the menus of North Indian restaurants and is popular as an appetiser. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 265 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 16.8 g fat, and 11.2 g carbohydrate. The gram flour batter contributes a low glycemic index of approximately 38, making it a lower-GI option compared to wheat-battered fish. The dish is a good source of fish-derived protein, omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, vitamin B12, selenium, and phosphorus. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the spice marinade and frying process, warranting caution for individuals managing hypertension. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI and high protein content make it a relatively suitable choice in controlled portions.

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Machli Amritsari by name; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN fried fish in batter and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central battered fried fish data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific Punjabi preparation.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (fried fish in besan batter) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175168 (fish, battered, fried). Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries and ICMR-NIN reference data; these values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered fish entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Machli Amritsari was found in approved sources. Serving size of 150 g is based on typical North Indian restaurant portion observation. The dish is typically halal as it contains fish and plant-based spices with no pork or alcohol in the standard recipe, though restaurant-specific preparation should be verified. Allergen note: gram flour (besan) is derived from chickpeas and is gluten-free by nature, but cross-contamination risk exists in restaurant kitchens.
